runs.interpretation: Medium-run elasticity of taxable income for top earners in the United States, combining both avoidance and real labor supply responses to changes in the net-of-tax rate.
citations: Saez, Slemrod, and Giertz (2012) - Elasticity of taxable income review
Feldstein (1995) - Taxable income response to taxation
Kopczuk (2005) - Tax avoidance and evasion among top earners
runs.reasoningSummary: The literature on taxable income elasticity for top earners has converged around a range of 0.25-0.65 in the medium run. My point estimate of 0.4 reflects that behavioral responses include both genuine labor supply adjustments and tax avoidance activities. Uncertainty is substantial due to identification challenges and variation across contexts, hence the wide distribution. The upper tail captures scenarios with greater avoidance sensitivity; the lower tail reflects more conservative labor supply estimates.

runs.interpretation: Medium-run elasticity of taxable income for top earners in the United States, combining both tax avoidance responses and real behavioral/labor supply responses to changes in the net-of-tax rate.
citations: Saez, Slemrod, and Giertz (2012) - comprehensive review of ETI research
Gruber and Saez (2002) - high-income ETI estimates
Piketty, Saez, and Stantcheva (2014) - optimal taxation and behavioral responses
runs.reasoningSummary: The empirical ETI literature for top earners centers around 0.3–0.5 for medium-run responses when both real and avoidance components are included. Historical estimates have ranged from near-zero to ~1.0+, with considerable heterogeneity by methodology and time period. A central estimate of 0.40 reflects the mainstream consensus that top earners show meaningful but moderate taxable income responsiveness, with substantial uncertainty reflecting methodological debates (whether avoidance is included, whether estimates capture supply or merely reporting shifts, and time horizons). The quantiles reflect this genuine disagreement in the literature.

Historical estimates have ranged from near-zero to ~1.0+, with considerable heterogeneity by methodology and time period. A central estimate of 0.40 reflects the mainstream consensus that top earners show meaningful but moderate taxable income responsiveness, with substantial uncertainty reflecting methodological debates (whether avoidance is included, whether estimates capture supply or merely reporting shifts, and time horizons). The quantiles reflect this genuine disagreement in the literature."}

runs.interpretation: Medium-run elasticity of taxable income for top earners in the United States, combining real labor supply responses and income shifting/avoidance responses to a 1 percent change in the net-of-tax rate.
citations: Saez, Slemrod, and Giertz (2012) on elasticity of taxable income for top earners
Gruber and Saez (2002) on the elasticity of taxable income with respect to marginal tax rates
Piketty, Saez, and Stantcheva (2014) on optimal taxation and behavioral responses
runs.reasoningSummary: The elasticity of taxable income for top earners typically ranges from 0.2 to 0.6 in the medium run when combining real labor supply adjustments and avoidance/income shifting. My central estimate of 0.35 reflects a balanced view: real responses are modest (ETI_real ≈ 0.1–0.15), while avoidance and reporting responses add substantially (0.2–0.3), with considerable uncertainty reflecting differences in methodology, time period, and tax reform contexts. The distribution is right-skewed because avoidance can amplify responses under certain conditions.

My central estimate of 0.35 reflects a balanced view: real responses are modest (ETI_real \u2248 0.1\u20130.15), while avoidance and reporting responses add substantially (0.2\u20130.3), with considerable uncertainty reflecting differences in methodology, time period, and tax reform contexts. The distribution is right-skewed because avoidance can amplify responses under certain conditions."}

runs.reasoningSummary: The ETI for top earners reflects both real labor supply adjustments and income-shifting/avoidance responses. Empirical estimates in the literature for medium-run horizons typically cluster in the 0.3–0.8 range when both margins are included. For top earners, avoidance responses are material, pushing estimates above pure labor supply elasticities (~0.1–0.3). A central estimate around 0.55 reflects moderate sensitivity and acknowledges substantial heterogeneity and method-dependence in the literature.

runs.reasoningSummary: ETI estimates for top earners have been highly heterogeneous, with significant variation driven by time horizon, whether avoidance channels are included, and identification methodology. I center at 0.5 as a reasonable midpoint reflecting the empirical range while accounting for uncertainty from different study designs and tax policy contexts. The upper tail is wider to reflect persistent disagreement in the literature and risk of underestimation.

runs.interpretation: Medium-run elasticity of taxable income for top earners in the United States, combining both tax avoidance and real behavioral responses to a 1 percent change in the net-of-tax rate.

runs.reasoningSummary: The elasticity of taxable income for top U.S. earners in the medium run appears to be moderate, incorporating both real labor supply responses and tax avoidance behavior. The academic consensus from major studies centers around 0.4–0.6, though estimates vary by methodology and time period. The distribution reflects uncertainty: lower bound (~0.15) acknowledges skeptics and mechanical responses; upper bound (~1.0) reflects high-avoidance scenarios. The median of 0.45 reflects a middle-ground estimate consistent with influential reviews.

runs.reasoningSummary: The literature on taxable income elasticity for high earners typically distinguishes between short-run and medium-run effects. The medium-run ETI for top earners, combining avoidance responses (which are relatively elastic) and labor supply adjustments (more modest), typically clusters in the 0.25–0.6 range. My point estimate of 0.4 reflects a central tendency with meaningful avoidance behavior while acknowledging real-response constraints. The uncertainty band (0.15–1.0) captures genuine disagreement in the literature about whether behavioral responses are concentrated in avoidance or real adjustments, and the degree of persistence.

runs.reasoningSummary: The elasticity of taxable income for top earners is a well-studied parameter in public finance. My belief reflects a moderate elasticity in the medium run, accounting for both real labor supply responses and income shifting/avoidance responses. The literature generally clusters in a range of 0.3–0.8 for combined effects, with substantial heterogeneity depending on methodology, time period, and income level. I center around 0.57 as a reasonable point estimate for top earners, with wide uncertainty reflecting methodological and empirical variation in the literature. The lower tail (0.20) allows for estimates emphasizing real responses only, while the upper tail (1.10) accommodates more aggressive estimates including avoidance and shifting.
